title: Lists / Arrays
summary: Store ordered values and access them by index.
refresher: Arrays hold ordered values with zero-based indexes. `.length` gives the count, `.push(value)` adds at the end, and `.pop()` removes the final item.

blocks.body: Arrays hold ordered values with zero-based indexes. `.length` gives the count, `.push(value)` adds at the end, and `.pop()` removes the final item. Store ordered values and access them by index.

An array stores an ordered set of values, but the array variable actually holds a reference to that collection. Methods such as `push`, `pop`, and `splice` change the existing array. Methods such as `slice`, `map`, and `filter` return another array, so save their result when you need it.

blocks.caption: Push adds a third item, while index one still selects the second course.
Copy before editing when the original must stay unchanged.

blocks.body: In the wrong version, `planned` and `courses` reach the same array, so both appear to gain a course. Spread reads the existing elements into a new outer array. For nested objects, that copy is shallow, so inner objects can still be shared.

blocks.title: Check the boundary
blocks.body: Const prevents assigning a different array to the variable, but it does not freeze the current array contents.

blocks.why: Length is three while the final valid index is two, so subtract one.
Push mutates the existing array, so const permits it and length becomes two.
